statement: If `f` and `g` commute, then for any polynomial `p`, both `ker (p(f))` and `range (p(f))`
lean_statement:
theorem ker_range_invtSubmodule_of_commute_polynomial
    {K V : Type*} [CommSemiring K] [AddCommMonoid V] [Module K V]
    (f g : Module.End K V) (hfg : f * g = g * f) (p : Polynomial K) :
    LinearMap.ker (Polynomial.aeval f p) ∈ g.invtSubmodule ∧
      LinearMap.range (Polynomial.aeval f p) ∈ g.invtSubmodule
proof:
are `g`-invariant.

Proof: from `f g = g f` it follows that `g` commutes with every power `f^n`, and hence with any
polynomial in `f`, i.e. with `p(f)`. Now if `x ∈ ker(p(f))`, then
`p(f) (g x) = g (p(f) x) = 0`, so `g x ∈ ker(p(f))`. If `y ∈ range(p(f))`, write `y = p(f) x`;
then `g y = g (p(f) x) = p(f) (g x)`, so `g y ∈ range(p(f))`. Thus both submodules are invariant.
